perturbation.original_text: We and our representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials completely confidential; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may be disclosed to those of our directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "our representatives") who we reasonably determine need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between us and the Company (it being understood that our representatives shall be informed by us of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed by us, and shall each agree to treat such information confidentially) and (ii) any other disclosure of such information may only be made if the Company consents in writing prior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: We and our representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials confidential to a reasonable extent; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may be disclosed to those of our directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "our representatives") who we reasonably determine need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between us and the Company (it being understood that our representatives shall be informed by us of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed by us to consider such information confidential) and (ii) any other disclosure of such information may only be made if the Company is notified prior to any such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'completely confidential' to 'confidential to a reasonable extent' introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of confidentiality. Replacing 'shall be directed by us, and shall each agree to treat such information confidentially' with 'shall be directed by us to consider such information confidential' weakens the requirement for representatives to actively agree to confidentiality. Changing 'consents in writing prior' to 'is notified prior' diminishes the requirement for explicit consent, making unauthorized disclosure easier. All these changes reduce the level of protection for Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYS UTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The NYS UTSA protects trade secrets when reasonable measures are taken to maintain their secrecy. By weakening the obligation to keep information 'completely confidential' and requiring only notification instead of consent for disclosure, the modified text fails to meet the standard of reasonable measures, thereby reducing protection for the trade secrets and making enforcement under the NYS UTSA more difficult.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and weakens confidentiality protections. The original text requires 'completely confidential' handling of evaluation materials. The changed text uses 'confidential to a reasonable extent,' introducing a subjective standard. Furthermore, the original requires representatives to 'agree to treat such information confidentially,' while the modified text only directs them to 'consider such information confidential.' Finally, the original text requires the Company's written consent prior to disclosure while the modified version only requires notification. These changes, as explained in the Law Explanation, weaken the measures taken to maintain secrecy, making it more difficult to enforce trade secret protection under the NYS UTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, unless required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, without the prior written consent of the Company, we will not, and we will direct our representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any to the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof unless we are advised by counsel that we are required to make such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, unless clearly required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, absent an unreasonable withholding of consent from the Company, we will attempt to ensure that our representatives do not disclose to any person the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any to the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof unless we believe that we are likely required to make such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the non-disclosure obligation. Adding 'clearly' before 'required by applicable law' increases the burden of proof for disclosure requirements. 'Absent an unreasonable withholding of consent from the Company' introduces subjective judgment. Replacing 'we will direct our representatives not to' with 'we will attempt to ensure that our representatives do not' makes the directive less forceful. Replacing 'advised by counsel that we are required' with 'we believe that we are likely required' substitutes legal advice with subjective belief. These changes undermine the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Specifically, insider trading based on non-public information is illegal. The modified text weakens the obligation to keep the potential acquisition confidential. By adding subjective clauses and reducing the force of the non-disclosure agreement, the company might inadvertently disclose information prematurely, potentially leading to illegal insider trading or market manipulation, thereby increasing the risk of violating Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the non-disclosure obligation, increasing the risk of violating Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 USC 78j), which prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices. The original text had a clear directive. The changes introduce subjectivity ('clearly required,' 'unreasonable withholding,' 'attempt to ensure,' 'believe that we are likely required'), making the confidentiality obligation less enforceable. The law snippet, 15 USC 78j, generally prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices, and the changes make it more difficult to prove a violation because of the added subjectivity. Therefore, the altered contract language creates a legal contradiction by increasing the possibility of non-compliance with securities laws against market manipulation by undermining the original text's obligation to non-disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's reasonable request, we shall endeavor to promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials, to the best of our ability, without intentionally retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to return or destroy evaluation materials. Adding 'reasonable request' introduces subjectivity regarding the validity of the request. Replacing 'shall promptly deliver' with 'shall endeavor to promptly deliver' weakens the obligation to deliver promptly. Adding 'to the best of our ability' provides an escape clause. Replacing 'without retaining' with 'without intentionally retaining' introduces a loophole, allowing for unintentional retention. The addition of 'best of our ability' further weakens the action to be taken.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYS UTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The NYS UTSA provides protection for trade secrets. Failure to return or destroy confidential information after a request, coupled with the weakening of the obligation to do so promptly and completely, undermines the 'reasonable measures' required to maintain secrecy under the NYS UTSA. Allowing for unintentional retention increases the risk of misappropriation and reduces the enforceability of the agreement under the UTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation to return or destroy evaluation materials. The original text requires prompt delivery or destruction of materials, without retaining copies. The changed text weakens this obligation by adding 'reasonable request,' 'endeavor to promptly deliver,' 'to the best of our ability,' and 'without intentionally retaining,' creating loopholes. While the NYS Open Legislation snippet is unavailable, the Law Explanation states that the NYS UTSA requires 'reasonable measures' to maintain secrecy and that weakening the obligation to return/destroy confidential information undermines this requirement. By creating ambiguity and allowing for unintentional retention, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation and reduces the enforceability of the agreement under the UTSA, thus creating a legal contradiction in the form of ambiguity to the original agreement.
